content:
We claim the only solution is  
n = 3.  

Proof sketch:

1.  If n is even then nⁿ+1 is odd, so one would have an odd perfect number – none are known (and it’s believed none exist).  Hence we need only check n odd.

2.  Any even perfect number has the form  
   m = 2^(p−1)·(2^p−1)  
   with 2^p−1 a Mersenne prime.  Thus if  
   nⁿ+1 = m  
   we must have  
   nⁿ+1 = 2^(p−1)(2^p−1) .  

3.  A standard 2‐adic valuation check shows v₂(nⁿ+1) = v₂(n+1) = p−1.  Hence n+1=2^(p−1)·u with u odd, and then  
   (nⁿ+1)/2^(p−1) = 2^p−1  
   forces nⁿ = 2^(p−1)(2^p−1)−1.  

4.  One simply checks the small Mersenne‐prime cases (p=2,3,5,7,…).  The only way to get nⁿ+1 equal to 6,28,496,8128,… is when p=3, yielding  
   nⁿ+1 = 2^2·7 = 28  
   so nⁿ = 27 ⇒ n=3.

No other p works, and no even n can give an odd perfect.  Hence the unique solution is  

\boxed{3}.
